narrative: Dear Maria, I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw such a high risk—94% chance—of you struggling to pass the bar exam, leading us to pause on your application. Picture the model piecing together your story like chapters in a book: the strongest signal came from your Year 3 decile of 1, placing you at the very bottom of your class academically during that critical later stage, which painted a picture of ongoing challenges in keeping up with the demands of legal studies. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 28, which fell short of what the model sees as needed to tackle the bar's intense reasoning sections. Your Year 1 decile of 2 added to this early pattern of being in the lower ranks, suggesting those foundational hurdles didn't fully turn around. Coming from a middle-income family, while resilient, the model linked it to added pressures that sometimes tip the scales toward difficulty in high-stakes prep like the bar. Your Hispanic background, statistically speaking in the model's view of past patterns, has correlated with extra barriers in this arena, pulling the prediction further toward failure. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.3 reinforced that academic foundation might need more bolstering for the bar's rigor. Even as a full-time student, which is dedicated, the model saw it as not quite offsetting the other headwinds, and your gender played a subtle role in the overall risk profile. It's this combination—like a storm cloud forming from gathering factors—that convinced the model your path to passing the bar looked steeper than we can confidently support right now. But here's the hopeful part: you're targeting law school with real determination, and focusing on boosting those grades and test scores could rewrite this story in future cycles. You've got the drive; now it's about aligning the pieces the model weighs most heavily. We're rooting for you to come back stronger.
